definitions.definition: to control a group of people, a country, or a situation
examples: I think we've chosen the right person to lead the expedition.

definitions.definition: (especially in sports or other competitions) to be in front, to be first, or to be winning
examples: After 30 minutes the challengers were leading by two goals.
With two laps to go Ngomo led by less than two seconds.
The Lions are leading the Hawks 28–9.

definitions.definition: to cause someone to do something, especially something bad
examples: The brochure led me to believe that the price included home delivery.
It's worrying that such a prominent politician is so easily led.
He was a weak man, led astray by ambition.

definitions.definition: to show the way to a group of people, animals, vehicles, etc. by going in front of them
examples: I don't know the way, so you'd better lead.
If you lead in the jeep, we'll follow behind on the horses.

definitions.definition: To lead a group of moving people or vehicles is to walk or drive in front of them
examples: The local youth band will lead the parade this weekend.
A large black hearse led the funeral procession.

definitions.definition: to take someone somewhere, by going with them
examples: She led them down the hall.

definitions.definition: to take hold of a person or animal and take him, her, or it somewhere
examples: She took the child by the hand and led him upstairs to bed.

definitions.definition: to show the way by going in front
examples: You've been there before - why don't you lead the way?

definitions.definition: to make more progress than other people in the development of something
examples: The company has been leading the way in network applications for several years.

definitions.definition: (especially of roads, paths, doors, signs, information, etc.) to go in a particular direction or have a particular result, or to allow or cause this
examples: There's a track that leads directly to the reservoir.
The door leads out onto a wide, shady terrace.
A narrow trail of blood led directly into the cave.

definitions.definition: to live a particular type of life

something you use, often unfairly, to try to persuade someone to do what you want
to move a bar or handle around a fixed point, so that one end of it can be pushed or pulled in order to control the operation of a machine or move a heavy or stiff object
examples: She levered up the drain cover.
He levered open the engine compartment and released a great cloud of blue smoke.

definitions.definition: If you lever someone or something to a place, you move them there with great physical effort
examples: He levered himself out of his chair with the usual difficulty and opened his arms wide.
Jim fought to pull himself up, levering his arms down.

definitions.definition: to change a situation in order to suit yourself, often using unfair methods
examples: The company gobbles up rivals or levers them out of the market.
They accused the companies of using political pressure to lever out competition.

definitions.definition: the action or advantage of using a lever
examples: Using ropes and wooden poles for leverage, they haul sacks of cement up the track.

definitions.definition: power to influence people and get the results you want
examples: If the United Nations had more troops in the area, it would have greater leverage.

definitions.definition: the relationship between the amount of money that a company owes to banks and the value of the company
the act of using borrowed money to buy an investment or a company
examples: With leverage, the investor's $100,000 buys $500,000 or more of stock if he wants.

definitions.definition: to use something that you already have in order to achieve something new or better
examples: We can gain a market advantage by leveraging our network of partners.

definitions.definition: to use borrowed money to buy an investment or company
examples: Home equity is invaluable if you leverage it to build wealth.

definitions.definition: to use money to get more money
examples: One of the easiest ways to leverage a charitable gift is to get your employer to match it.

definitions.definition: A leveraged company or organization owes a large amount of money in relation to its value
examples: The company is highly leveraged and struggling with interest payments.

definitions.definition: A leveraged deal or investment has been paid for using borrowed money.

definitions.definition: past simple and past participle of lever
to move a bar or handle around a fixed point, so that one end of it can be pushed or pulled in order to control the operation of a machine or move a heavy or stiff object
examples: She levered up the drain cover.
He levered open the engine compartment and released a great cloud of blue smoke.

definitions.definition: If you lever someone or something to a place, you move them there with great physical effort
examples: He levered himself out of his chair with the usual difficulty and opened his arms wide.
Jim fought to pull himself up, levering his arms down.

definitions.definition: to change a situation in order to suit yourself, often using unfair methods

They accused the companies of using political pressure to lever out competition.

definitions.definition: the period between birth and death, or the experience or state of being alive
examples: Life's too short to worry about money!
I'm not sure I want to spend the rest of my life with him.

mainly US spelling of likeable
to enjoy or approve of something or someone
examples: I like your new haircut.
Do you like fish?
I like it when a book is so good that you can't put it down.
I quite like wine but I could live without it.
He's very well-liked (= popular) at work.
I like the way he just assumes we'll listen to him when he doesn't take in a word anyone else says! (= I don't like it and it annoys me.)
I don't like upsetting people.
He likes to spend his evenings in front of the television.
He likes his steak well-done.

definitions.definition: to show that you think something is good on a social networking website by clicking on a symbol or the word 'like'
examples: Like us on Facebook!
More than 200 people liked my post.

definitions.definition: used to say politely that you want something
examples: I think I'd like the soup for my starter.
I'd like to go to Moscow.
I would like to say a big thank you to everyone who's helped to make our wedding such a special occasion!

definitions.definition: used in requests
examples: I'd like one of the round loaves, please.
I'd like to book a seat for tonight's performance.
I'd like you to send this for me, please.
I would like the whole lot finished by the weekend.

definitions.definition: similar to; in the same way or manner as
examples: He looks like his brother.
She's very much like her mother (= she is similar in appearance or character).
Is Japanese food like Chinese?
I've got a sweater just like that.
Her hair was so soft it was like silk.
You're acting like a complete idiot!
She sings like an angel!
Like I said (= as I have already said), I don't wear perfume.
Like most people (= as most people would), I'd prefer to have enough money not to work.
It feels/seems like (= it seems to me) ages since we last spoke.
There's nothing like a good cup of coffee (= it's better than anything)!

definitions.definition: in a way that suggests
examples: It looks like I'm going to be in the office until late tonight.
It looks like rain (= I think it is going to rain).
It sounds to me like you ought to change jobs.
You look like you've just got out of bed!
She acts like she's stupid!

definitions.definition: typical or characteristic of
examples: That's just like Maisie to turn up half an hour late to her own party!
It's not like you to be so quiet - are you all right, my love?

definitions.definition: such as
examples: She looks best in bright, vibrant colours, like red and pink.

definitions.definition: used before you describe how you were feeling or what you said when something happened to you
examples: Then I saw how late it was and I'm like, so upset.
He started shouting at me and I'm like, "What's your problem? I'm on your side!"

definitions.definition: used in conversation as a pause or to emphasize an adjective
examples: He's, like, really friendly - someone you can talk to.
If there's nothing you can do to change the situation, it's like - why bother?

definitions.definition: a person, thing, or group similar in character or quality to the one mentioned
examples: Boxing hasn't seen the likes of Muhammad Ali since he retired.
He was a very great actor - we won't see his like again.
He described a superlative meal, the like of which he'd never eaten before.
They're competing with the likes of Microsoft.

definitions.definition: and similar things
examples: There's a big sports hall for tennis and badminton and such like.

definitions.definition: the things that someone enjoys
examples: The star lists his likes as "my new Porsche, my girlfriend, and staying up all night."

definitions.definition: not for the type of people mentioned
examples: First-class travel is for rich people - it's not for the likes of us.

definitions.definition: (on a page on a social networking website) an act of showing that you think something is good by clicking a button
examples: My new profile picture got 100 likes.

definitions.definition: similar to, or the same as, something else

definitions.definition: The like poles (= ends) of a magnet (= an object that both attracts and pushes away iron and steel objects) are the ends which have the same charge (= negative or positive type of energy) and repel (= push away) each other

definitions.definition: small in size or amount
examples: It came in a little box.
a little dog/nose/room
A little old man came into the room.
He gave a little smile.
It'll only take a little while to clear up the kitchen.

definitions.definition: a small amount of food or drink
examples: I always like to have a little something around eleven o'clock in the morning.

definitions.definition: a present that is not of great value
examples: I want to buy a little something to give to Val when I visit her in hospital.

definitions.definition: young
examples: When you were little your hair was really curly.
She was my little (= younger) sister and I took care of her.
Her little boy (= her young son) isn't well.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an opinion that is being given about something or someone
examples: That was a nice little suit she was wearing.
It's not a bad little restaurant, is it?
He's a nasty little man.

definitions.definition: not very important or serious
examples: I had a little problem with my car, but it's been fixed now.
It's often the little things that count the most.
Can I have a little word (= a short discussion about something not very important) with you?

definitions.definition: not much or enough
examples: There seems little hope of a ceasefire.
They have very little money.
There's so little choice.

definitions.definition: a small amount of something
examples: This sauce needs a little salt.

Can I give you a little advice?

definitions.definition: a small amount
examples: I could only hear a little of what they were saying.
He does as little as possible at work.
There's not much flour left but you're welcome to the/what little there is.

definitions.definition: an amount that is not much or not enough
examples: We did very little on Sunday.
Very little of what he said made any sense to me.

The little we do know about the people who lived here suggests they had a very sophisticated society.

definitions.definition: slightly
examples: I was a little bit worried by what she said.

definitions.definition: slowly or gradually
examples: Little by little she came to understand why he had behaved the way he did.

examples: She slept very little that night.

definitions.definition: present participle of lure
to persuade someone to do something or go somewhere by offering them something exciting
examples: She was lured into the job by the offer of a high salary.

definitions.definition: to wait or move in a secret way so that you cannot be seen, especially because you are about to attack someone or do something wrong
examples: Someone was lurking in the shadows.

definitions.definition: (of an unpleasant feeling or quality) to exist although it is not always noticeable
examples: Danger lurks around every corner.
